executive_verdict.primary_failure_mode: The runtime now has explicit legacy-layer labeling and a hard user-crontab ownership guard, but blocked-channel recovery still runs two schedules against one owner path without a separate verifier boundary.
executive_verdict.most_urgent_fix: Decide whether blocked-channel recovery should collapse to one owner schedule or split into distinct deep-review/followup entrypoints with separate evidence.

findings.title: Blocked-channel recovery still has two schedules driving one owner path
findings.mechanism: `blocked-channel-deep-review` and `blocked-channel-followup` are explicitly unblocker-owned, but both still invoke the same unittest + `agents/unblocker/run.py` path and still do not emit distinct verifier artifacts or separate runtime contracts.
findings.root_cause: The ownership cleanup renamed the schedules and clarified their boundary faster than the runtime split was implemented.
findings.recommended_fix: Either collapse both schedules into one unblocker owner loop or split deep-review and followup into distinct entrypoints with separate artifacts so overlap becomes intentional and auditable.

findings.title: Legacy disabled jobs are now clearly deactivated, but they still remain in the scheduler store as audit-history clutter
findings.mechanism: The disabled mixed-product/content/docs legacy jobs no longer read like active authorities, but keeping them in the live Gateway store still adds inventory surface that future audits must mentally filter.
findings.root_cause: Historical cleanup preserved disabled jobs for traceability instead of archiving or pruning them out of the runtime store.
findings.recommended_fix: Once audit-history retention is no longer needed, archive or remove the legacy disabled jobs instead of keeping them in the live scheduler inventory.

ordered_fix_plan.goal: Normalize blocked-channel recovery into one explicit runtime contract
ordered_fix_plan.why_now: It remains the highest-risk overlap in the live recovery topology.
ordered_fix_plan.expected_effect: Cleaner ownership, less duplicate recovery churn, and clearer verifier expectations.

ordered_fix_plan.goal: Prune or archive disabled legacy cron jobs once audit-history retention is no longer required
ordered_fix_plan.why_now: The prompts are now safe, but the extra inventory still adds avoidable cognitive load and future drift surface.
ordered_fix_plan.expected_effect: Less stale scheduler surface and fewer chances for legacy prompts to confuse future audits.

ordered_fix_plan.goal: Keep the user-crontab ownership invariant strict and only add allowlist entries for explicitly justified OS-cron owners
ordered_fix_plan.why_now: This run converted a previously soft governance expectation into a hard runtime check.
ordered_fix_plan.expected_effect: Hidden scheduler drift fails fast instead of waiting for manual discovery.

repairs_applied_this_run.change: Normalized five legacy disabled jobs plus the already-disabled docs self-heal entry so their descriptions/messages explicitly mark them as legacy/deactivated audit context rather than active authorities.
Added a hard user-crontab ownership invariant. Any meaningful user crontab entry is now treated as architecture drift unless explicitly allowlisted.
Reran the loop-integrity audit. The refreshed artifact now includes `user-crontab-ownership` with status `ok` and confirms no stray user-crontab entries were present at 2026-05-21 10:56 UTC.

independent_verification.summary: A fresh isolated verifier confirmed that disabled legacy jobs now read as deactivated audit context, that the loop-integrity audit enforces the new user-crontab invariant, and that the latest integrity artifact shows no stray crontab entries.

highest_risk_unresolved_issue.title: Shared blocked-channel code path with split schedules but no split verifier contract
highest_risk_unresolved_issue.why: The ownership names are now clean, but the runtime still has two cadences feeding one recovery implementation without distinct proof boundaries.
